amber.description: Catalysts, regulatory proteins, molecular machines, receptors

black.description: Terminal products, cellular outcomes, equilibrium states, system endpoints

darkSkyBlue.description: Biochemical reactions, transport, modifications, transcription, translation

green.description: External signals, nutrient availability, environmental conditions, initial states

lightCyan.description: Metabolites, molecular complexes, cellular states, compounds

purple.description: Multi-component requirements, signal integration, convergence points

yellow.description: Decision points with alternative pathways (yes/no branching)

description: The general starvation and stress response in E. coli is mediated by the alternative sigma factor RpoS (σS or σ38), which redirects RNA polymerase to transcribe >500 genes involved in stress protection, stationary phase survival, and starvation adaptation. RpoS is regulated at multiple levels: transcriptional (by cAMP-CRP), translational (by small RNAs and RNA-binding proteins), and post-translational (by ClpXP protease degradation). Under favorable growth conditions, RpoS is rapidly degraded; under stress (nutrient limitation, osmotic stress, pH extremes, stationary phase), RpoS accumulates and activates genes for DNA protection, oxidative stress defense, osmoprotection, and metabolic remodeling. The RssB adaptor protein targets RpoS to ClpXP for degradation, and this targeting is inhibited by ppGpp and phosphorylation. This flowchart illustrates the multi-layered regulation of RpoS and its role as a master regulator of the general stress response.

name: General Starvation Response (RpoS/σS)
notes: RpoS represents a paradigm for multi-layered gene regulation, with control at transcriptional, translational, and post-translational levels. The small RNA regulation by DsrA/RprA was groundbreaking in establishing sRNAs as major regulators. RpoS is called the master regulator of general stress as it controls >500 genes and responds to diverse stresses. The RssB-ClpXP degradation system ensures rapid RpoS turnover during growth while allowing accumulation under stress. RpoS-deficient strains are viable but show reduced survival in stationary phase and multiple stress sensitivities. This system is highly conserved among enteric bacteria.
scientificAccuracy: This flowchart is based on decades of research on RpoS regulation and function. All regulatory mechanisms (transcriptional, translational, post-translational), target genes, and physiological roles have been extensively validated. The model represents the paradigm for bacterial general stress response and stationary phase adaptation.

sources.relevance: Comprehensive review of RpoS regulation at multiple levels
sources.title: The RpoS sigma factor of Escherichia coli: synthesis and degradation

sources.authors: Majdalani N, Cunning C, Sledjeski D, Elliott T, Gottesman S
sources.relevance: Discovery of small RNA regulation of RpoS translation
sources.title: Small RNAs, DsrA and RprA, control the synthesis of the stationary phase sigma factor in Escherichia coli

sources.authors: Becker G, Klauck E, Hengge-Aronis R
sources.relevance: Mechanism of RpoS degradation by ClpXP protease via RssB adaptor
sources.title: The adaptor protein RssB regulates proteolysis of the stationary-phase sigma factor RpoS

sources.authors: Kvint K, Nachin L, Diez A, Nyström T
sources.relevance: Role of ppGpp in stabilizing RpoS and inducing stationary phase genes
sources.title: ppGpp-dependent stationary phase induction of genes on Escherichia coli
